narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 44—well above the typical 37—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 48% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize that risk for the program. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like early academic rankings, family background, and test scores that correlate with bar success. The three biggest influences on your specific outcome were your LSAT, your third-year school decile ranking of 2 (far below the average 5.5), and your first-year decile of 2 (also well under the typical 5.7). Your strong LSAT was the most powerful positive factor, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass by signaling sharp analytical skills that often predict bar success. However, your lower decile rankings in Years 1 and 3 pulled in the opposite direction, as they suggest challenges sustaining top performance in a competitive school environment, which historically raises failure risks for students in similar positions. Even with your lower-middle family income and full-time student status—both common among applicants—the model weighed those early grades heavily against your LSAT strength. Compared to typical students who pass, you're ahead on LSAT but trail significantly on in-school deciles, where stronger rankings often anchor predictions of bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real barriers; it's the model highlighting where your profile matches higher-risk patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even modest gains there could flip future predictions. Building a stronger undergraduate GPA trajectory, now at 2.7 versus the 3.2 average, through targeted prep or community college refreshers might also help. Reach out to admissions for feedback, explore bridge programs designed for underrepresented applicants like yourself, and prepare rigorously for the bar early—many in your position succeed by addressing these exact gaps. Your LSAT shows you're capable; focusing here can open doors.
